summary: I was born at the Cherokee Indian Hospital on March 3rd, 1971. It’s right here on the Qualla Boundary where I was raised. That hospital marks the beginning of my life and connection to this community.

I grew up on the Qualla Boundary, which is the Cherokee homeland where I started my journey. It’s a small town where in winter you could run through it and see hardly anyone, but in summer it’s bustling with tourists. It’s where my family lives and where I learned the traditional ways before I started traveling and dancing.

Rick Youngblood is like a dad to me. He’s been there for me through a lot, teaching me traditional ways and helping me get out and travel beyond the Qualla Boundary. I started dancing with him when I was about twenty-one, and he introduced me to the Daniel Boone Festival and many other experiences that opened my world.

The Daniel Boone Festival has been a big part of my life since I first went there at twenty-one with Rick. It’s a place where families come together, much like in Barbourville, Kentucky, where people return home to reunite. My family and I perform there, and it’s a joyful, relaxing event filled with dancing, food like chicken and dumplings, and community bonding. It’s more than a festival; it’s a connection to family and homeland.

Barbourville, Kentucky, is where the Daniel Boone Festival takes place, and it feels like home to many Cherokee people who have moved away for work. People come back there to see family and enjoy the festival. I’ve spent time there performing and even riding my bike around the town when I stayed at Harold’s during school shows.

I am Cherokee and proud to represent my tribe through dance and culture. The Cherokee community is like family to me, and even those who aren’t Cherokee but travel and dance with us are accepted as family. Our traditions and homeland on the Qualla Boundary are central to who I am and how I connect with others at events like the Daniel Boone Festival.

Walker Calhoun and his family have a history of attending the Daniel Boone Festival, just like my family and others from the Cherokee community. They are part of the tradition of coming together at this event, which connects many Cherokee families across generations and places.

Richard Crow is part of the Cherokee families who have long attended the Daniel Boone Festival, alongside families like Walker Calhoun’s and ours. He represents the deep connections Cherokee people maintain with the festival as a homeland gathering place.

Knox County, Kentucky, is where I performed school shows as part of our outreach before the Daniel Boone Festival. I remember going to the high school and elementary schools there, sometimes arriving by bike from where I stayed at Harold’s. It was a way to share Cherokee culture and dancing with the local community.

Harold’s is where I stayed when I traveled to Barbourville for school shows and the Daniel Boone Festival. It was a base for me to change and prepare for performances, and from there I’d bike around town to meet people and explore. Harold’s was an important part of my experience traveling off the reservation.

When Walmart finally came to Barbourville, it was a big deal, kind of like how it would be if one opened here on the Qualla Boundary. It changed the local shopping scene and was something people were really excited about. It’s a marker of how the town has evolved over time.
